title: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall

items.subtopic: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET
items.title: Understanding Hemostasis: The Role of Platelets and Vessel Walls
items.content: Hemostasis is a dynamic process where platelets and vessel walls are crucial. Upon vascular injury, platelets adhere to von Willebrand factor (VWF) and collagen in the subendothelium, initiating activation. This activation is further influenced by shear forces from blood flow and endothelial inflammation. Activated platelets facilitate coagulation factor activation, leading to more platelet activation and fibrin formation.
keyPoints: Platelets adhere to VWF and collagen upon vascular injury.
Shear forces and endothelial inflammation affect platelet activation.
Activated platelets are central to coagulation factor activation and fibrin formation.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET

items.subtopic: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET
items.title: Regulation of Platelet Production and Function
items.content: Platelet production is regulated by thrombopoietin (TPO), synthesized in the liver and other organs, with increased synthesis during inflammation. TPO binds to receptors on platelets and megakaryocytes, facilitating its removal from circulation. A decrease in platelet mass elevates TPO levels, stimulating production. Platelets have a lifespan of 7–10 days and are physiologically active despite being anucleate.
keyPoints: Normal platelet count: 150,000–450,000/μL.
Thrombopoietin (TPO) regulates platelet production.
Platelets have a lifespan of 7–10 days and are physiologically active.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET

items.title: Endothelial Role in Thrombosis and Hemostasis
items.content: The endothelium plays a critical role in regulating blood fluidity and hemostasis by balancing its antithrombotic/prothrombotic and vasodilatory/vasoconstrictor properties. It can rapidly switch from an antithrombotic to a prothrombotic surface, influencing coagulation, fibrinolysis, and platelet activation.
keyPoints: Endothelial cells cover approximately six tennis courts in total surface area.
The endothelium controls vascular permeability, blood cell interactions, inflammatory response, and angiogenesis.
Under normal conditions, the endothelium is antithrombotic but can become prothrombotic when stimulated.
Endothelial-derived vasodilators (e.g., nitric oxide) are platelet inhibitors; vasoconstrictors (e.g., endothelin) activate platelets.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THE VESSEL WALL | THROMBOCYTOPENIA

items.title: Diagnosing Pseudothrombocytopenia
items.content: Pseudothrombocytopenia is an in vitro artifact caused by platelet agglutination due to antibodies when blood is collected with EDTA. It should be considered in patients without clear causes for thrombocytopenia, and confirmed by re-evaluating the platelet count using alternative anticoagulants or unanticoagulated blood.
keyPoints: Pseudothrombocytopenia results from platelet agglutination due to antibodies (IgG, IgM, IgA) in EDTA-anticoagulated blood.
It is important to rule out pseudothrombocytopenia by examining a peripheral blood smear and rechecking the platelet count using sodium citrate or heparin tubes, or unanticoagulated blood.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THE VESSEL WALL | THROMBOCYTOPENIA

items.title: When to Examine Bone Marrow in Thrombocytopenic Patients
items.content: Bone marrow examination is recommended for patients presenting with isolated thrombocytopenia who are older than 60 years or do not respond to initial therapy, due to the possibility of myelodysplasia.
keyPoints: Myelodysplasia can present with isolated thrombocytopenia.
Bone marrow examination is indicated for patients over 60 with isolated thrombocytopenia or those unresponsive to initial therapy.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Platelet Count Thresholds
items.content: Petechiae appear when platelet counts are markedly decreased, typically below 5000–10,000/μL. Wet purpura indicates a higher risk of life-threatening hemorrhage.
keyPoints: Platelet count required to maintain vascular integrity is approximately 5000–10,000/μL.
Petechiae appear when platelet counts are markedly decreased.
Wet purpura denotes increased risk of life-threatening hemorrhage.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia
items.content: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is characterized by a non-severe decrease in platelet count, typically not below 20,000/μL, and increases the risk of thrombosis rather than bleeding.
keyPoints: Nadir platelet counts in HIT rarely fall below 20,000/μL.
HIT is associated with an increased risk of thrombosis, not bleeding.
Pathogenesis involves antibodies to a complex of PF4 and heparin.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.title: Timing and Risk Factors for HIT
items.content: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) typically develops after heparin exposure within 5–14 days, but can occur earlier if there are preexisting anti-heparin/PF4 antibodies from prior exposure. Delayed-onset HIT and spontaneous HIT are rare occurrences.
keyPoints: Most patients develop HIT after 5–14 days of heparin exposure.
HIT occurs before 5 days with prior heparin exposure due to preexisting anti-heparin/PF4 antibodies, which disappear ~100 days post-exposure.
Delayed-onset HIT can occur several days after stopping heparin; spontaneous HIT occurs without prior heparin exposure.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach and Laboratory Testing for HIT
items.content: The '4Ts' scoring system aids in diagnosing HIT by evaluating thrombocytopenia, timing of platelet count drop, thrombosis/thrombotic sequelae, and other causes. Laboratory testing should be reserved for those at intermediate or high risk, using immunoassays or platelet activation assays.
keyPoints: '4Ts' scoring system: evaluates thrombocytopenia, timing of platelet drop, thrombosis, and other causes.
Immunoassays detect anti-heparin/PF4 antibodies but have low specificity; common in post-cardiopulmonary bypass patients (~50%).
Serotonin release assay has higher specificity than ELISA for HIT diagnosis.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 2)

items.title: Management of HIT: Discontinue Heparin and Initiate Alternative Anticoagulation
items.content: In patients diagnosed with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT), immediate discontinuation of heparin is crucial. Transition to alternative anticoagulants, such as direct thrombin inhibitors like argatroban or bivalirudin, is recommended due to the risk of thrombosis even after stopping heparin.
keyPoints: Prompt discontinuation of heparin upon HIT diagnosis
Use of DTIs (e.g., argatroban) as effective alternatives
Avoid LMWH in HIT treatment due to cross-reactivity
Anticoagulation should be considered even without thrombosis
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.title: Thrombosis and Anticoagulation Duration in HIT Patients
items.content: Patients with HIT are at high risk for thrombosis, necessitating imaging studies like lower extremity duplex Doppler. Anticoagulation should be continued for 3–6 months if thrombosis is present; otherwise, it should last until platelet recovery.
keyPoints: Imaging recommended to evaluate for thrombosis
Anticoagulation duration: 3-6 months with thrombosis, until platelet recovery without
Thrombosis risk persists at least 1 month post-diagnosis
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.title: Warfarin and HIT: Risks and Recommendations
items.content: Initiating warfarin alone in HIT can precipitate thrombosis, especially venous gangrene. It should be overlapped with a DTI, fondaparinux, or DOAC after resolving thrombocytopenia.
keyPoints: Warfarin alone may cause thrombosis in HIT
Overlap warfarin with DTI/fondaparinux/DOAC post-thrombocytopenia resolution
Start warfarin only when the prothrombotic state lessens
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.title: When to Treat ITP and Initial Management Strategies
items.content: Treatment of Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) is not mandatory for all patients. It is particularly unnecessary in those with platelet counts above 30,000/μL as they do not exhibit increased mortality from thrombocytopenia. Initial outpatient treatment can be considered for patients without significant bleeding symptoms or severe thrombocytopenia (<5000/μL), using agents like prednisone (1 mg/kg) or dexamethasone (40 mg/d for 4 days). Rh₀(D) immune globulin therapy is also an option, dosed at 50–75 μg/kg for Rh-positive patients. IVIgG, dosed at 1–2 g/kg over 1-5 days, can be used especially in postsplenectomized patients.
keyPoints: Platelet counts >30,000/μL do not increase mortality risk related to thrombocytopenia.
Initial outpatient treatment options include prednisone (1 mg/kg) or dexamethasone (40 mg/d for 4 days).
Rh₀(D) immune globulin therapy is used at 50–75 μg/kg, only in Rh-positive patients.
IVIgG dosed at 1–2 g/kg over 1-5 days, more effective than anti-Rh₀(D) in postsplenectomized patients.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.title: Management of Severe ITP and Chronic ITP Options
items.content: In cases of severe ITP or bleeding symptoms, hospital admission is necessary. Treatment involves high-dose glucocorticoids combined with IVIgG or anti-Rh₀(D) therapy, along with additional immunosuppressive agents if needed. For chronic ITP, TPO receptor agonists (TPO-RA) like romiplostim and eltrombopag are preferred over splenectomy due to fewer side effects and better quality of life outcomes. Post-splenectomy vaccinations against encapsulated organisms are recommended.
keyPoints: Severe ITP requires hospital admission with high-dose glucocorticoids, IVIgG, or anti-Rh₀(D) therapy.
TPO receptor agonists (e.g., romiplostim, eltrombopag) are preferred for chronic ITP over splenectomy.
Post-splenectomy vaccinations against pneumococcus, meningococcus, and Haemophilus influenzae are recommended.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.subtopic: THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of TTP: The Role of ADAMTS13 Activity Levels
items.content: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) is characterized by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, renal failure, neurologic findings, and fever. A critical diagnostic marker for TTP is the level of ADAMTS13 activity. Deficiency or antibodies to ADAMTS13 lead to the persistence of ultra-large von Willebrand factor (VWF) multimers, promoting platelet adhesion and aggregation.
keyPoints: ADAMTS13 activity levels <10% are diagnostic of TTP.
Plasma exchange significantly improves prognosis, reducing mortality from 85–100% to 10–30%.
TTP is more common in women, patients with HIV infection, and pregnant women.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.subtopic: THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME
items.title: Differentiating TTP from Other Causes of MAHA: Importance of Coagulation Tests
items.content: In differentiating thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) from other causes of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, such as disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), it is important to note that TTP and HUS typically present with normal prothrombin time (PT) and activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT). In contrast, DIC is characterized by elevated PT and aPTT due to consumption of clotting factors.
keyPoints: Normal PT and aPTT are characteristic in TTP or HUS.
Elevated PT and aPTT indicate DIC rather than TTP/HUS.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.title: Prompt Diagnosis and Treatment of TTP with Therapeutic Plasma Exchange (TPE)
items.content: In patients presenting with new thrombocytopenia, especially when accompanied by renal insufficiency or other classic signs of TTP, it is crucial to obtain specific laboratory data to differentiate from conditions like DIC and confirm MAHA. The mainstay treatment for TTP is therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE), which should be continued until the platelet count normalizes and hemolysis resolves for at least 2 days.
keyPoints: Laboratory tests include PT, aPTT, CBC with platelet count and peripheral smear, ADAMTS13 activity, LDH, bilirubin, haptoglobin, direct antiglobulin assay.
TPE is the mainstay treatment for TTP.
Continue TPE until platelet count normalizes and signs of hemolysis resolve for at least 2 days.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.title: Management of Relapses in TTP and Use of Adjunctive Therapies
items.content: TTP has a significant relapse rate, with 25–45% of patients experiencing relapse within 30 days post-remission. Early initiation of treatment for relapses is recommended before confirmatory lab results are available. The addition of rituximab to initial therapy can decrease the risk of relapse. Caplacizumab should be started in patients with ADAMTS13 <10% or high clinical probability, as it reduces mortality and care burden.
keyPoints: 25–45% of TTP patients relapse within 30 days post-remission.
12–40% experience later relapses; more frequent in those with severe ADAMTS13 deficiency at presentation.
Initiate treatment for relapses before confirmatory lab results are available.
Rituximab decreases risk of relapse when added to initial therapy.
Caplacizumab recommended for patients with ADAMTS13 <10% or high clinical probability.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.title: Management and Prognosis of HUS
items.content: Treatment for Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome (HUS) is primarily supportive. In cases associated with diarrhea, about 40% of children may need dialysis support, though the overall mortality remains below 5%. For non-diarrheal HUS, mortality increases to approximately 26%. Plasma exchange or infusion does not generally alter outcomes in HUS or atypical HUS (aHUS), except for patients with factor H antibodies. In aHUS, anticomplement therapy can be effective in resolving the condition and preserving renal function.
keyPoints: Supportive treatment is primary for HUS.
~40% of children with diarrhea-associated HUS may require dialysis.
Overall mortality <5% for diarrhea-associated HUS; ~26% for non-diarrheal HUS.
Plasma exchange or infusion generally does not alter outcomes in HUS/aHUS, except in patients with factor H antibodies.
Anticomplement therapy is effective in aHUS for resolution and renal function preservation.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION

items.title: Etiology and Clinical Implications of Thrombocytosis
items.content: Thrombocytosis can result from iron deficiency, inflammation/cancer/infection (reactive thrombocytosis), or myeloproliferative disorders like essential thrombocythemia. Evaluation should include ruling out underlying inflammation and malignancy, as well as iron deficiency. Notably, extremely high platelet counts (>1.5 million) are often linked to bleeding risks due to acquired von Willebrand disease from platelet-VWF binding.
keyPoints: Thrombocytosis causes: iron deficiency, inflammation/cancer/infection (reactive), myeloproliferative disorders.
Evaluate for underlying inflammation and malignancy; rule out iron deficiency.
High platelet counts (>1.5 million) increase bleeding risk due to acquired von Willebrand disease.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION

items.title: Management Strategies for Severe Platelet Dysfunction
items.content: Severe platelet dysfunction often necessitates platelet transfusion. To minimize alloimmunization risks, human leukocyte antigen-matched single donor platelets should be used when indicated. Recombinant factor VIIa (rFVIIa) is approved for use in Glanzmann's thrombasthenia and Bernard Soulier syndrome to prevent alloimmunization and antireceptor antibody formation.
keyPoints: Platelet transfusion may be required for severe platelet dysfunction.
Use human leukocyte antigen-matched single donor platelets to reduce alloimmunization risk.
rFVIIa is FDA approved for Glanzmann's thrombasthenia and Bernard Soulier syndrome.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Inherited Disorders of Platelet Dysfunction

items.title: Treatment Options for Milder Bleeding Symptoms in Platelet Disorders
items.content: Milder bleeding symptoms associated with platelet disorders can often be managed with desmopressin (DDAVP), which increases plasma VWF and factor VIII levels. DDAVP may also directly affect platelet function. For mucosal bleeding, antifibrinolytic therapy using tranexamic acid or $ \varepsilon $-aminocaproic acid can be employed alone or alongside DDAVP or platelet transfusion.
keyPoints: Desmopressin (DDAVP) is effective for milder bleeding symptoms in platelet disorders.
DDAVP increases plasma VWF and factor VIII levels and may affect platelet function directly.
Antifibrinolytic therapy can be used alone or with DDAVP/platelet therapy for mucosal bleeding.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Inherited Disorders of Platelet Dysfunction

items.title: Prevalence and Initial Presentation of Type 1 VWD
items.content: Type 1 von Willebrand Disease (VWD) is the most common form, accounting for at least 80% of cases. It presents with mucosal bleeding symptoms such as excessive bruising and epistaxis, often manifesting later in childhood.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 1 in 1000 to 1 in 10,000 individuals
Type 1 VWD accounts for at least 80% of cases
Common symptoms: mucosal bleeding (e.g., bruising, epistaxis), heavy menstrual bleeding
Symptoms often manifest later in childhood
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE

items.title: Diagnosis Considerations for VWD with Low VWF Levels
items.content: Not all patients with low von Willebrand factor (VWF) levels exhibit bleeding symptoms. Diagnosis should consider bleeding symptoms and/or family history, especially in type O blood individuals where normal VWF ranges overlap with diagnostic criteria.
keyPoints: Diagnosis of VWD requires bleeding symptoms or family history
Type O blood has lower VWF levels (approx. half of AB blood type)
Normal range for type O overlaps with VWD diagnostic range
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Type 2B VWD
items.content: Type 2B VWD involves gain-of-function variants leading to increased platelet binding, resulting in higher ADAMTS13 cleavage and clearance. Patients may have reduced platelet counts.
keyPoints: Type 2B: Increased platelet-binding due to gain-of-function variants
Results in higher ADAMTS13 cleavage and VWF clearance
Platelet count is usually modestly reduced
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE

items.title: Management of Type 1 VWD with DDAVP
items.content: DDAVP is effective for treating type 1 Von Willebrand Disease (VWD) by releasing von Willebrand factor (VWF) and Factor VIII from endothelial stores. It can be administered intravenously, intranasally, or subcutaneously. The peak activity occurs approximately 30 minutes after IV administration and 2 hours post-intranasal use.
keyPoints: DDAVP dose: 0.3 μg/kg IV; two squirts (one in each nostril) for >50 kg, one squirt for <50 kg
Peak activity: ~30 min IV, 2 h intranasally
Use DDAVP if response is a twofold increase in lab values to >50% for at least 4 hours
DDAVP can be used for minor to moderate bleeding risk procedures with additional doses every 12–24 h as needed
Major side effect: hyponatremia, especially in very young and old; advise fluid restriction post-dose
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Von Willebrand Disease

items.title: Use of Antifibrinolytic Therapy in VWD
items.content: Antifibrinolytic agents like tranexamic acid (TXA) or $ \varepsilon $-aminocaproic acid are crucial for managing mucosal bleeding in VWD. They are particularly beneficial for heavy menstrual bleeding, postpartum hemorrhage, and as prophylaxis during dental procedures.
keyPoints: Tranexamic acid dose: 1300 mg every 8 hours
Useful for heavy menstrual bleeding, postpartum hemorrhage, dental procedure prophylaxis
Can be used with DDAVP or factor concentrate for dental extractions, tonsillectomies, and prostate procedures
Contraindicated in upper urinary tract bleeding due to risk of ureteral obstruction
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Von Willebrand Disease

items.title: Vascular Damage from Infections and Immune Responses
items.content: Infectious agents such as rickettsiae and SARS-CoV-2 can directly damage endothelial cells, leading to vascular complications. Additionally, immune responses during acute febrile illnesses may result in similar vascular damage.
keyPoints: Rickettsial infections like Rocky Mountain spotted fever replicate in endothelial cells causing damage.
SARS-CoV-2 infects and damages endothelial cells contributing to COVID-19 pathogenicity.
Vascular purpura is more common with monoclonal gammopathies such as Waldenström's macroglobulinemia, multiple myeloma, and cryoglobulinemia.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · DISORDERS OF THE VESSEL WALL

items.title: Characteristics of Immunoglobulin A Vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein Purpura)
items.content: Immunoglobulin A vasculitis is a self-limited vasculitis primarily affecting children and young adults, characterized by IgA deposition in small vessels leading to increased vascular permeability and localized hemorrhage.
keyPoints: Occurs often after upper respiratory infections or due to allergies.
Patients present with purpuric rash, polyarthralgias/arthritis, abdominal pain, and hematuria.
Renal impairment may occur despite normal coagulation tests.
Glucocorticoids provide symptomatic relief but do not alter the course of illness.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · DISORDERS OF THE VESSEL WALL

items.subtopic: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET
items.question: Which molecule primarily mediates platelet adhesion to the exposed subendothelium after vascular injury?
items.explanation: Platelets adhere primarily through von Willebrand factor (VWF) after vascular injury.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET

items.subtopic: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET
items.question: What is the major regulator of platelet production?
items.explanation: The hormone thrombopoietin (TPO) is the major regulator of platelet production.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET

items.subtopic: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET
items.question: What is the average lifespan of circulating platelets?
items.explanation: Platelets have an average lifespan of 7–10 days in circulation.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cause of thrombocytopenia?
items.explanation: Thrombocytopenia is caused by decreased bone marrow production, sequestration, or increased platelet destruction, not endothelial cell dysfunction.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THE VESSEL WALL | THROMBOCYTOPENIA

items.question: What is pseudothrombocytopenia?
options: A true decrease in platelet count due to bone marrow failure
An artifact caused by platelet agglutination in EDTA-anticoagulated blood
Increased destruction of platelets in the spleen
Decreased production of platelets in the bone marrow
items.explanation: Pseudothrombocytopenia is an artifact resulting from platelet agglutination when calcium content is decreased by EDTA.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THE VESSEL WALL | THROMBOCYTOPENIA

items.question: Which anticoagulant is associated with pseudothrombocytopenia?
items.explanation: Pseudothrombocytopenia is associated with EDTA, the anticoagulant in purple top tubes used for CBCs.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THE VESSEL WALL | THROMBOCYTOPENIA

items.question: In which patient population is it recommended to examine the bone marrow in cases of isolated thrombocytopenia?
options: Patients younger than 60 years without response to initial therapy
All patients with isolated thrombocytopenia regardless of age
Patients older than 60 years or those not responding to initial therapy
Only patients with a family history of thrombocytopenia
items.explanation: The bone marrow should be examined in patients presenting with isolated thrombocytopenia who are older than 60 years of age or who do not respond to initial therapy.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical platelet count required to maintain vascular integrity in the microcirculation?
items.explanation: A platelet count of approximately 5000–10,000 is required to maintain vascular integrity in the microcirculation.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.question: What type of thrombocytopenia is associated with an increased risk of life-threatening hemorrhage due to blood blisters on the oral mucosa?
items.explanation: Wet purpura, blood blisters that form on the oral mucosa, are thought to denote an increased risk of life-threatening hemorrhage in the thrombocytopenic patient.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.question: Which infections are most commonly associated with disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) in thrombocytopenic patients?
items.explanation: Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) is most commonly seen in patients with systemic infections with gram-negative bacteria.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.question: Which type of heparin is more commonly associated with the development of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)?
options: Neither, HIT is not related to heparin
items.explanation: HIT is more commonly associated with unfractionated heparin (UFH) than low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH).
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 2)

items.question: When does HIT most frequently occur after initial exposure to heparin?
options: Only after all heparin has been stopped
items.explanation: Most patients develop HIT after exposure to heparin for 5–14 days.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 2)

items.question: What is the specificity of immunoassays used in detecting anti-heparin/PF4 antibodies?
items.explanation: The test has a low specificity for the diagnosis of HIT, especially post-surgery with cardiopulmonary bypass.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following anticoagulants is recommended for use in patients with HIT who are acutely ill?
options: Direct thrombin inhibitor (DTI) such as argatroban
items.explanation: An intravenous DTI should be used in acutely ill patients with HIT.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.question: What is the recommended imaging study for evaluating thrombosis in patients diagnosed with HIT?
items.explanation: Imaging studies such as lower extremity duplex Doppler imaging are recommended to evaluate for thrombosis in HIT.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.question: Which of the following is a potential complication if warfarin is introduced alone in the setting of HIT?
options: Venous gangrene due to clotting activation
items.explanation: Introduction of warfarin alone in HIT may precipitate thrombosis, particularly venous gangrene.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.question: Which treatment is usually used for VITT syndrome to block platelet activation?
items.explanation: Treatment for VITT syndrome usually involves intravenous gamma globulin to block platelet activation.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.question: Which of the following treatments for ITP is specifically indicated only for Rh-positive patients?
items.explanation: Rh₀(D) immune globulin must be used only in Rh-positive patients because its mechanism involves limited hemolysis.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.question: What is the recommended initial treatment for ITP patients without significant bleeding symptoms or severe thrombocytopenia?
options: Outpatient single-agent therapy with prednisone or dexamethasone
items.explanation: Initial treatment can be outpatient using single agents like prednisone or a course of dexamethasone.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.question: Which therapy is noted to have more efficacy than anti-Rh₀(D) in postsplenectomized patients?
items.explanation: IVIgG has more efficacy than anti-Rh₀(D) in postsplenectomized patients.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.subtopic: THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME
items.question: Which laboratory findings are characteristic of thrombotic microangiopathies such as TTP and HUS?
options: Elevated prothrombin time (PT) and activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT)
Decreased lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and elevated haptoglobin
Absence of fragmented RBCs on peripheral blood smear
items.explanation: The PT and aPTT are characteristically normal in TTP or HUS.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.subtopic: THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME
items.question: What is the diagnostic threshold for ADAMTS13 activity levels to diagnose TTP?
items.explanation: ADAMTS13 activity levels of <10% are diagnostic of TTP.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.subtopic: THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME
items.question: Which condition is associated with episodic development of TTP due to congenital absence of ADAMTS13?
items.explanation: Individuals with a congenital absence of ADAMTS13 develop TTP only episodically, including during first pregnancy.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.question: Which laboratory test is used to evaluate for evidence of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA) in suspected TTP?
options: CBC with platelet count and peripheral smear
items.explanation: A CBC with platelet count and peripheral smear is used to evaluate for evidence of MAHA.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.question: What is the mainstay treatment for TTP?
items.explanation: Therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) remains the mainstay of treatment for TTP.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.question: Which medication is recommended by guidelines to be started in individuals with diagnostic ADAMTS13 levels (<10%)?
items.explanation: Guidelines recommend starting caplacizumab in individuals with diagnostic ADAMTS13 levels (<10%).
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.subtopic: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION
items.question: In patients with atypical Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome (aHUS), which treatment has been shown to improve or preserve renal function?
items.explanation: Anticomplement therapy has efficacy in resolution of aHUS and improving or preserving renal function.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION

items.subtopic: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION
items.question: Which condition is associated with an increased risk of bleeding due to markedly elevated platelet counts?
items.explanation: Patients with markedly elevated platelet counts, usually seen in the setting of a myeloproliferative disorder like essential thrombocythemia, have an increased risk of bleeding.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION

items.subtopic: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION
items.question: Which inherited platelet function disorder is characterized by the absence of the Gp IIb/IIIa receptor?
items.explanation: Glanzmann's thrombasthenia is characterized by the absence of the platelet Gp IIb/IIIa receptor.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION

items.question: Which treatment is FDA approved for use in Glanzmann's thrombasthenia to avoid platelet alloimmunization?
items.explanation: Recombinant factor VIIa is FDA approved for use in Glanzmann's thrombasthenia to avoid platelet alloimmunization.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Inherited Disorders of Platelet Dysfunction

items.question: What medication may increase plasma VWF and factor VIII levels, potentially improving milder bleeding symptoms?
items.explanation: Desmopressin (DDAVP) increases plasma VWF and factor VIII levels, potentially improving milder bleeding symptoms.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Inherited Disorders of Platelet Dysfunction

items.question: For which type of bleeding symptoms is antifibrinolytic therapy particularly used?
items.explanation: Antifibrinolytic therapy is particularly used for mucosal bleeding symptoms.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Inherited Disorders of Platelet Dysfunction

items.question: Which of the following statements best describes the inheritance pattern and influencing factors for von Willebrand Disease (VWD)?
options: VWD is inherited in an X-linked recessive manner, with no known environmental influences.
The inheritance of VWD is autosomal, but several factors such as blood type and hormonal status can modulate VWF levels and bleeding symptoms.
VWD is primarily influenced by dietary habits and physical activity, with minimal genetic contribution.
VWD inheritance is mitochondrial, affecting only maternal lineage.
items.explanation: The inheritance of VWD is autosomal, but several factors such as blood type and hormonal status can modulate VWF levels and bleeding symptoms.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE

items.question: In which type of von Willebrand Disease are patients most likely to experience a significant reduction in factor VIII (FVIII) levels?
items.explanation: In Type 3 VWD, patients have virtually no VWF protein and usually FVIII levels <10%.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE

items.question: What is the primary cause of bleeding symptoms in type 1 von Willebrand Disease?
options: Decreased clearance of VWF leading to excessive binding with platelets.
A parallel decrease in VWF protein, function, and FVIII levels.
Gain-of-function DNA variants increasing VWF binding to platelets.
items.explanation: In type 1 VWD, there is a parallel decrease in VWF protein, function, and FVIII levels.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE

items.question: Which of the following types of von Willebrand Disease involves an acquired form due to cardiac valvular disease?
items.explanation: Acquired VWD can be seen in patients with cardiac valvular disease, such as Heyde's syndrome.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE

items.question: Which route of administration for DDAVP results in peak activity approximately 2 hours after dosing?
items.explanation: The peak activity when given intranasally is approximately 2 hours.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Von Willebrand Disease

items.question: What is the major side effect of DDAVP?
items.explanation: The major side effect of DDAVP is hyponatremia due to decreased free water clearance.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Von Willebrand Disease

items.question: In which setting are antifibrinolytic agents contraindicated?
items.explanation: Antifibrinolytic agents are contraindicated in the setting of upper urinary tract bleeding due to the risk of ureteral obstruction.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Von Willebrand Disease

items.question: Which condition is characterized by acute febrile illnesses that can result in vascular damage due to immune complexes containing viral antigens?
items.explanation: Vascular purpura may occur in patients with polyclonal gammopathies, often due to immune complexes containing viral antigens.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · DISORDERS OF THE VESSEL WALL

items.question: What is a common symptom of Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) in children and young adults?
items.explanation: Henoch-Schönlein purpura is characterized by a purpuric rash on the extensor surfaces of the arms and legs.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · DISORDERS OF THE VESSEL WALL

items.question: Which inherited disorder involves abnormal telangiectatic capillaries leading to frequent bleeding episodes, primarily from the nose?
items.explanation: Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia is characterized by abnormal telangiectatic capillaries causing frequent bleeding episodes, primarily from the nose.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · DISORDERS OF THE VESSEL WALL

items.subtopic: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET
items.statement: The spleen can hold up to two-thirds of the total platelet count without affecting the circulating platelet number.
items.explanation: Approximately one-third, not two-thirds, of platelets reside in the spleen.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET

items.subtopic: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET
items.statement: Activated platelet glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptor is crucial for platelet aggregation.
items.explanation: The activation of the glycoprotein IIb/IIIa (GpIIb/IIIa) receptor leads to platelet aggregation.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall | THE PLATELET

items.statement: Endothelial cells are involved in regulating blood fluidity and hemostasis.
items.explanation: The endothelium regulates blood fluidity and hemostasis through its antithrombotic/prothrombotic and vasodilatory/vasoconstrictor properties.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THE VESSEL WALL | THROMBOCYTOPENIA

items.statement: Pseudothrombocytopenia can be confirmed by repeating the platelet count in blood collected into EDTA tubes.
items.explanation: To confirm pseudothrombocytopenia, a platelet count should be determined using sodium citrate or heparin, not EDTA.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THE VESSEL WALL | THROMBOCYTOPENIA

items.statement: Classic drug-dependent antibodies result in thrombocytopenia only when the drug is present.
items.explanation: Classic drug-dependent antibodies react with specific platelet surface antigens and cause thrombocytopenia only when the drug is present.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.statement: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) typically results in severe bleeding.
items.explanation: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is not associated with bleeding and increases the risk of thrombosis.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.statement: HIT can occur after exposure to both unfractionated heparin and low-molecular-weight heparin.
items.explanation: HIT can indeed occur with either type of heparin.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 2)

items.statement: The serotonin release assay has higher sensitivity than the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) for detecting HIT.
items.explanation: The serotonin release assay has lower sensitivity but higher specificity compared to ELISA.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 2)

items.statement: Low molecular weight heparins are safe and recommended in the treatment of HIT.
items.explanation: LMWHs cross-react with HIT antibodies and should not be used.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.statement: Anticoagulation is recommended for patients with HIT even in the absence of thrombosis.
items.explanation: Due to high rates of thrombosis, anticoagulation should be considered even without thrombosis.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.statement: Patients with platelet counts >30,000/μL have increased mortality related to thrombocytopenia.
items.explanation: Patients with platelet counts >30,000/μL do not appear to have increased mortality related to the thrombocytopenia.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.statement: TPO receptor agonists are now generally used less frequently compared to splenectomy for chronic ITP.
items.explanation: TPO-RAs are preferred over splenectomy due to fewer side effects and better quality of life.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.subtopic: THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME
items.statement: The introduction of plasma exchange has not significantly changed the mortality rate in TTP.
items.explanation: Plasma exchange markedly improved prognosis, decreasing mortality from 85–100% to 10–30%.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.subtopic: THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME
items.statement: TTP is more common in patients with HIV infection and pregnant women.
items.explanation: TTP is indeed more common in these populations.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · THROMBOTIC THROMBOCYTOPENIC PURPURA AND HEMOLYTIC-UREMIC SYNDROME

items.statement: The addition of rituximab to initial therapy decreases the risk of relapse in TTP.
items.explanation: Rituximab is added to reduce the risk of relapse.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.statement: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome (HUS) is characterized by thrombocytopenia, acute renal failure, and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia.
items.explanation: These are the characteristics of HUS.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura

items.subtopic: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION
items.statement: Plasma exchange has been shown to alter the overall course in Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome (HUS).
items.explanation: Plasma exchange has not been shown to alter the overall course in HUS, except in patients with antibodies to factor H.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION

items.subtopic: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION
items.statement: Thrombocytosis is almost always due to iron deficiency, inflammation, or an underlying myeloproliferative process.
items.explanation: Thrombocytosis is usually due to iron deficiency, inflammation, cancer, infection (reactive thrombocytosis), or a myeloproliferative process.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION

items.statement: Platelet transfusion is always the first line of treatment for inherited platelet dysfunction.
items.explanation: Platelet transfusion is not always the first line; other treatments like rFVIIa and DDAVP are considered to avoid alloimmunization.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Inherited Disorders of Platelet Dysfunction

items.statement: Recombinant factor VIIa can be used in Bernard Soulier syndrome.
items.explanation: Recombinant factor VIIa is FDA approved for use in Bernard Soulier syndrome.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Inherited Disorders of Platelet Dysfunction

items.statement: Patients with type O blood have higher VWF protein levels compared to those with AB blood type.
items.explanation: Patients with type O blood have VWF protein levels of approximately one-half those of patients with AB blood type.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE

items.statement: Type 2N von Willebrand Disease is characterized by a gain-of-function variant affecting FVIII binding.
items.explanation: Type 2N VWD involves variants in the VWF gene that affect binding of FVIII, leading to decreased FVIII levels.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE

items.statement: DDAVP is recommended for all patients with VWD before using it.
items.explanation: It is recommended that patients with VWD be tested with DDAVP to assess their response before using it.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Von Willebrand Disease

items.statement: Antifibrinolytic therapy can be used alone or in combination for the prevention of mucosal bleeding.
items.explanation: Antifibrinolytic therapy is important either alone or in an adjunctive capacity, particularly for the prevention or treatment of mucosal bleeding.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Von Willebrand Disease

items.statement: Patients with scurvy develop skin bleeding due to a deficiency in vitamin C needed for collagen synthesis.
items.explanation: Vitamin C is essential for synthesizing hydroxyproline, a component of collagen, and its deficiency leads to painful perifollicular skin bleeding.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · DISORDERS OF THE VESSEL WALL

items.statement: Henoch-Schönlein purpura often results in abnormal coagulation tests.
items.explanation: All coagulation tests are normal in Henoch-Schönlein purpura, despite the presence of localized hemorrhage.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · DISORDERS OF THE VESSEL WALL

items.assertion: Infection-induced thrombocytopenia can result from both decreased platelet production and increased platelet destruction.
items.reason: Viral and bacterial infections affect both platelet production and survival, with immune mechanisms also playing a role in some cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.assertion: Drug-induced thrombocytopenia is always associated with severe bleeding.
items.reason: Many drugs cause isolated thrombocytopenia and can be confirmed with positive laboratory testing.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; drug-induced thrombocytopenia is not always associated with severe bleeding, as seen in heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 1)

items.reason: Laboratory testing for HIT has low specificity and variable sensitivity, making clinical assessment crucial.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why HIT remains a clinical diagnosis.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Thrombocytopenia (part 2)

items.assertion: Warfarin therapy can precipitate thrombosis in HIT.
items.reason: This is due to clotting activation and reduced levels of proteins C and S.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why warfarin may cause thrombosis.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.assertion: Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are FDA-approved for use in HIT.
items.reason: Rivaroxaban, a DOAC, has reported efficacy in HIT treatment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as DOACs are not FDA-approved for HIT; however, rivaroxaban does have some reported experience.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia

items.subtopic: Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION
items.assertion: Patients with reactive thrombocytosis have an increased risk of thrombotic events.
items.reason: Thrombocytosis in response to acute or chronic inflammation has not been clearly associated with an increased thrombotic risk.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because reactive thrombocytosis due to inflammation does not increase thrombotic risk, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Hemolytic-Uremic Syndrome | THROMBOCYTOSIS | QUALITATIVE DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION

items.assertion: Desmopressin (DDAVP) can be used to treat milder bleeding symptoms associated with inherited platelet disorders.
items.reason: DDAVP increases plasma VWF and factor VIII levels, which may improve platelet function.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why DDAVP is used for milder bleeding symptoms.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · Inherited Disorders of Platelet Dysfunction

items.assertion: Patients with type 2B von Willebrand Disease have an increased platelet count.
items.reason: Type 2B VWD results from gain-of-function DNA variants that increase binding of VWF to platelets, leading to their clearance and a reduced platelet count.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; type 2B VWD leads to a modestly reduced platelet count due to increased clearance.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE

items.assertion: Type 1 von Willebrand Disease often manifests with heavy menstrual bleeding.
items.reason: Type 1 VWD is the most common form and involves a parallel decrease in VWF protein, function, and FVIII levels, leading to mucosal bleeding symptoms like heavy menstrual bleeding.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall · VON WILLEBRAND DISEASE
